title: Choosing paper for different uses
subtitle: Matching paper weight, surface, sizing, and grain to calligraphy, watercolour, bookbinding, and printing
excerpt: The right paper depends on how it will be used. A sheet good for calligraphy is a poor choice for watercolour, and text paper ideal for bookbinding behaves badly in a printer. Weight, surface texture, sizing level, and grain direction each matter differently depending on the task.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), chapter on paper qualities and their relation to use. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/oldpapermak00hunt. Hunter distinguishes paper made for different trade uses and the properties required for each.

glossaryTerms.definition: Grams per square metre: the standard unit for describing paper weight. A single sheet of 80 gsm paper and one of 300 gsm may have identical dimensions, but the heavier sheet is thicker, stiffer, and holds more water before distorting.

A paper surface finish produced by pressing the dried sheet through heated metal rollers. The result is a very smooth, hard surface with minimal texture, suitable for fine-line pen work and detailed illustration. Abbreviated HP.

A paper surface finish that has not been hot-pressed. In watercolour papers, 'NOT' (Not hot-pressed) indicates a slight tooth or texture intermediate between rough and HP. Abbreviated NOT or CP (Cold Pressed).

content.text: Four things determine what a paper is good for: weight (in

), surface texture, sizing level, and grain direction. A sheet suited to one task often performs poorly in another. The most common mistake is buying one general-purpose paper and expecting it to do everything.

Calligraphy, whether broad-edge or pointed pen, requires a sheet that holds ink at the surface without bleeding. The key properties are: adequate surface sizing, low texture, and a smooth or slightly textured surface that does not snag the nib. For broad-edge scripts (italic, foundational, Gothic), a

or calendered sheet at 100 to 150 gsm is the standard. Cartridge paper at 130 gsm is the standard practice surface. Genuine parchment (vellum) and prepared paper treated with alum-gelatine sizing give the hardest writing surface and the sharpest stroke edges, but are unnecessary for practice work.

For pointed pen copperplate and Spencerian work, surface smoothness is critical. Any paper with visible texture will catch the nib tines on upstrokes, splaying them or depositing ink erratically. Hot-pressed illustration board or smooth marker paper gives the cleanest strokes.

Watercolour paper must absorb water without immediate cockle and without dissolving. The standard weight is 300 gsm (also marked 140 lb). Below 200 gsm, a wet wash causes severe cockling unless the sheet is stretched or mounted before use. Surface texture is a matter of style: rough paper holds visible granulation;

(cold-pressed) is the most widely used all-purpose surface; hot-pressed gives a flat wash with no granulation and suits precise botanical illustration. Cotton-rag paper holds more water and dries more slowly than wood-pulp paper, giving more working time for wet-in-wet techniques.

Text block paper must have its grain direction parallel to the spine. Cross-grained text paper fights every page-turn and causes the binding to warp as humidity changes. Weight for text pages is typically 70 to 100 gsm: light enough to fold cleanly for signatures, heavy enough to be opaque and to take writing without showthrough. Acid-free or alkaline-reserve paper extends the life of the book considerably; wood-pulp paper with no acid treatment yellows and becomes brittle within decades.

If the book will be written in, paper sizing matters for the intended ink. Heavily sized paper resists water-based ink; lightly sized or unsized paper absorbs it quickly. Fountain-pen inks require moderate sizing; iron-gall inks work on slightly absorbent surfaces as well as very smooth ones.
Paper for home printing and photocopying

Laser printers and inkjet printers need different paper. Laser printers use heat to fuse toner. They need smooth, well-sized paper with low moisture content. High texture or damp paper causes toner adhesion problems. Inkjet printers deposit liquid ink. They need some paper absorbency to stop puddling, but not so much that ink bleeds. Standard 80 gsm office paper works in both. Handmade paper is unsuitable for either. Its uneven thickness causes feed jams, and unsized handmade paper smears inkjet output.

Calligraphy: 100 to 150 gsm, smooth surface, well-sized. Watercolour: 300 gsm minimum, NOT or rough surface, cotton rag preferred. Bookbinding text: 70 to 100 gsm, grain parallel to spine, acid-free. Printing: 80 gsm, smooth or mildly textured, standard commercial sizing. Handmade paper is best used for projects designed around its properties: as the text block of a hand-written journal, as a decorative endpaper, or as the printmaking substrate rather than as an all-purpose substitute for commercial paper.
